text.title: Distributed and Resilience Patterns
text.summary: Resilience patterns (Circuit Breaker, Bulkhead, Retry, Timeout, Fallback) prevent cascade failure in distributed systems. Data patterns (CQRS, Event Sourcing, Saga, Outbox, Repository) manage consistency and state in multi-service architectures.

rule.statement: Apply resilience patterns defensively at every external service boundary. The minimum set for any production service call is: Timeout + Retry with exponential backoff + Circuit Breaker. Add Bulkhead when one downstream service must not starve others.
rule.rationale: Without timeouts, slow downstream services exhaust thread pools. Without circuit breakers, retries amplify load on an already-failing service. Without bulkheads, one slow dependency cascades to all consumers sharing the same thread pool.

list.item: Circuit Breaker: 3 states (Closed/Open/Half-Open); opens after N failures; probes recovery via half-open — prevents cascade failure to failing services
Bulkhead: separate thread pools or semaphores per downstream service — isolates one failure from starving others
Retry: limited retries with exponential backoff and jitter — only for idempotent calls; never retry non-idempotent writes without idempotency keys
Timeout: hard deadline on every external call — prevent slow callee from holding caller's thread indefinitely
Fallback: degrade gracefully when a dependency fails — return cached result, empty list, or feature-flagged default

rule.statement: In microservices, use Saga for distributed transactions across services. Use the Outbox pattern to guarantee that a database write and a message publish succeed or fail together. Use CQRS when read and write models must scale or evolve independently.
rule.rationale: Distributed 2-phase commit (2PC) is impractical in microservices due to availability coupling. Sagas replace 2PC with compensating transactions. The Outbox pattern replaces dual-write (DB + message broker) with an atomic local transaction.

list.item: Saga: long-running transaction across services; two flavors: Choreography (events) and Orchestration (central coordinator); each step has a compensating transaction for rollback
Outbox: write domain event to outbox table in same DB transaction as the domain record; relay process publishes from outbox to broker — guarantees exactly-once publish
CQRS: separate Command (write) model from Query (read) model; read model updated by events from write model; enables independent scaling and read-optimized projections
Event Sourcing: store all state changes as an append-only event log; current state = replay of events; pairs with CQRS for read projections
Repository: abstract data access behind a collection-like interface; domain layer depends only on the interface, infrastructure provides the implementation

antipattern.description: Dual-write: writing to database and message broker in two separate operations
antipattern.reason: If the service crashes between the DB write and the broker publish, the two systems diverge permanently. Use the Outbox pattern instead.
